summary: Gruber compares the iPad's first seven years to the Mac's, building on Dr. Drang's observation that iPad software has advanced surprisingly slowly. While the Mac had self-hosting development tools, mature multitasking, and killer productivity apps like PageMaker and Photoshop by its seventh year, the iPad remains primarily a casual-use device. Gruber argues the iPad's software limitations — not hardware — explain why owners feel no urgency to upgrade. He contends that the Mac's continued relevance is precisely what allows iOS to remain simple, and that the iPad needs to become more like a touch-based Mac rather than a big iPhone to drive upgrade cycles.
key_points: By its seventh year, the Mac was self-hosting for software development and had killer productivity apps that pushed hardware limits; the iPad has neither
The iPad excels at casual use — reading, video, writing — but none of those tasks demand newer, more powerful hardware, explaining slow upgrade cycles
A general-purpose computing platform should be self-sufficient, meaning you can write software for the platform on the platform itself
The Mac's existence allows iOS to stay light and simple by carrying the 'baggage' of complex tasks — this relationship has barely changed since 2010
Apple Pencil creates genuine hardware demand for iPad Pro, but drawing is a niche that won't drive mass upgrades
money_quotes: Serious general-purpose personal computing platforms should be self-sufficient, by which I mean that you should be able to write software for the platform on the platform itself.
The bigger problem for the iPad is that there are few productivity tasks, period, where iPad is hardware-constrained.
If Apple wants it to be a platform where people are tempted to replace them more frequently, I think the software story needs to change.
For that to happen, the iPad experience needs to be less like a big iPhone, and more like a touch-based Mac.
The fact that that doesn't seem to be Apple's goal for the iPad is the reason why I, unlike many others, continue to feel confident about the future of the Mac.
It's the heaviness of the Mac that allows iOS to remain light.
The central conceit of the iPad is that it's a portable computer that does less — and because it does less, what it does do, it does better, more simply, and more elegantly.
Here we are, six years later, and the Mac's role in the iOS world is only slightly less essential now than it was then.
key_insight: The iPad's slow upgrade cycle is a software problem, not a hardware one — and the Mac's continued necessity for serious productivity work is both the iPad's limitation and its own lifeline.
